CBB.name: Cassava Bacterial Blight
CBB.description: A bacterial disease causing leaf spots, wilting, and stem rot. Can lead to complete plant death if untreated.
symptoms: Angular leaf spots with yellow halos
Wilting of leaves starting from tips

treatments.details: Uproot and burn all infected plants to prevent spread. Do not compost.
Source certified, disease-free stem cuttings from trusted suppliers
Spray copper oxychloride or copper hydroxide on healthy plants near infected areas
Rotate with non-host crops for at least 2 seasons

prevention: Disinfect tools between plants (10% bleach solution)
CBB.timeToRecovery: Not recoverable - prevent spread to healthy plants

CBSD.name: Cassava Brown Streak Disease
CBSD.description: A viral disease transmitted by whiteflies, causing brown streaks in stems and roots, making tubers unmarketable.
symptoms: Brown necrotic patches in tuber flesh

treatments.details: Uproot and destroy infected plants within 24-48 hours of detection
Apply imidacloprid or thiamethoxam insecticides to control whitefly population
Only use certified CBSD-free stem cuttings from accredited sources
Regular field inspections and removal of symptomatic plants

CGM.name: Cassava Green Mottle
CGM.description: A viral disease causing mottled green patterns on leaves, reducing photosynthesis and yield.
symptoms: Light and dark green mosaic patterns on leaves

treatments.details: Remove and destroy plants showing severe symptoms
Apply systemic insecticides to control whiteflies and other vectors
Select cuttings from healthy, disease-free mother plants

CGM.timeToRecovery: No recovery - manage to prevent spread

CMD.name: Cassava Mosaic Disease
CMD.description: The most widespread and damaging cassava disease in Africa, transmitted by whiteflies, causing severe yield losses.
symptoms: Yellow and green mosaic patterns on leaves

treatments.details: Remove and burn all infected plants to stop virus spread
Apply neem-based or synthetic insecticides (imidacloprid) to control whitefly vectors
Replant with resistant varieties like TMS30572, TME204, or local resistant cultivars
Remove all cassava debris and volunteer plants from field

prevention: Remove infected plants early (within first 2 months)
Maintain isolation from other cassava fields (100m minimum)

Healthy.name: Healthy Cassava
Healthy.description: Your cassava plant appears healthy with no signs of disease. Continue good agricultural practices.
prevention: Monitor for early signs of disease
Use certified planting material in future
Healthy.recommendation: Maintain current good practices. Monitor weekly for any disease symptoms.

Tomato___Bacterial_spot.name: Bacterial Spot
Tomato___Bacterial_spot.description: A bacterial disease causing leaf spots and fruit lesions, reducing marketability and yield.
symptoms: Small dark spots on leaves with yellow halos

treatments.details: Spray copper hydroxide or copper oxychloride every 7-10 days
treatments.timing: Start at first sign of disease

treatments.details: Prune and destroy infected leaves and fruits
Increase plant spacing and prune lower leaves
Purchase certified pathogen-free seeds or transplants

Tomato___Early_blight.name: Early Blight
Tomato___Early_blight.description: A common fungal disease causing target-spot lesions on leaves, stems, and fruits.
symptoms: Dark brown spots with concentric rings (target pattern)

treatments.details: Use chlorothalonil, mancozeb, or azoxystrobin every 7-14 days
Prune lower leaves and any infected foliage
Apply organic mulch to prevent soil splash
Ensure proper soil drainage and avoid waterlogging

prevention: Use drip irrigation instead of overhead
Rotate crops (avoid tomatoes/potatoes in same spot for 3 years)
Stake plants for better air circulation

Tomato___Late_blight.name: Late Blight
Tomato___Late_blight.description: The most destructive tomato disease, can destroy entire crop within days during favorable conditions.
symptoms: Large irregular brown-black lesions on leaves
White fuzzy growth on leaf undersides (in humid conditions)
Firm brown lesions on green fruits

treatments.details: Apply systemic fungicides (metalaxyl, cymoxanil) immediately at first sign
Remove entire severely infected plants to prevent spread
Reduce soil moisture and humidity around plants
Harvest any marketable fruits before disease spreads

prevention: Plant resistant varieties (e.g., Mountain Merit, Plum Regal)
Monitor weather - disease thrives in cool, wet conditions
Apply preventive fungicides before symptoms appear
Destroy volunteer tomato and potato plants
Tomato___Late_blight.timeToRecovery: 3-5 days to plant death if untreated; 2-3 weeks with aggressive treatment

Tomato___Leaf_Mold.name: Leaf Mold
Tomato___Leaf_Mold.description: A fungal disease primarily affecting greenhouse tomatoes, causing leaf yellowing and mold growth.
symptoms: Yellow spots on upper leaf surface
Olive-green to brown fuzzy mold on leaf undersides

treatments.details: Use chlorothalonil or copper-based fungicides
Prune and dispose of affected foliage
Improve greenhouse ventilation, reduce humidity below 85%
Use fans in greenhouse, increase plant spacing

prevention: Use resistant varieties (genes Cf-1 to Cf-11)
Remove lower leaves to improve airflow

Tomato___healthy.name: Healthy Tomato
Tomato___healthy.description: Your tomato plant is healthy with no visible disease symptoms. Continue your current care practices.
prevention: Support plants with stakes or cages
Tomato___healthy.recommendation: Excellent! Your plant is healthy. Continue current practices and monitor regularly for any changes.
